text: The transformer model relies entirely on attention mechanisms to draw global dependencies between input and output sequences without using recurrent networks.
Multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions improving model capacity.
Scaled dot-product attention computes the dot products of the query with all keys divided by the square root of the dimension to stabilize gradients.
Positional encoding is added to the input embeddings to give the model information about the relative or absolute position of tokens in the sequence.
The encoder maps an input sequence of symbol representations to a sequence of continuous representations used by the decoder to generate output.
BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers of the transformer.
BERT uses masked language modeling where some input tokens are randomly masked and the model learns to predict the original vocabulary item from context.
Next sentence prediction is a binary classification task used in BERT pre-training to help the model understand relationships between sentence pairs.
BERT fine-tuning adds a single output layer on top of the pre-trained model to perform downstream tasks like question answering and text classification.
The BERT base model has 12 transformer layers 768 hidden units and 12 attention heads totaling 110 million parameters trained on BooksCorpus and Wikipedia.
GPT uses a unidirectional transformer trained with a language modeling objective to predict the next token given all previous tokens in the sequence.
The model is fine-tuned on downstream tasks using task-specific input transformations that convert structured inputs into ordered token sequences.
GPT-2 demonstrates that language models can perform tasks like translation and summarization without task-specific training through zero-shot generalization.
GPT-3 scales language model pre-training to 175 billion parameters and shows strong few-shot performance across a wide range of NLP benchmarks.
In-context learning allows GPT models to adapt to new tasks using only a few examples provided in the prompt without updating any model parameters.
Retrieval augmented generation combines a retriever that fetches relevant documents with a generator that produces answers grounded in retrieved evidence.
The retriever uses dense passage retrieval with a bi-encoder architecture to find the most relevant chunks from a large document corpus efficiently.
RAG reduces hallucination in language models by grounding generation in retrieved documents ensuring answers are supported by real evidence from the corpus.
The generator in RAG conditions on both the input query and the retrieved documents to produce faithful and informative answers with citations.
RAG can be updated with new knowledge by simply updating the document index without expensive retraining of the underlying language model parameters.
LLaMA is a collection of foundation language models ranging from 7B to 65B parameters trained exclusively on publicly available datasets for reproducibility.
The model uses rotary positional embeddings and grouped query attention to improve computational efficiency and reduce memory usage during inference.
LLaMA-2 introduces supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback to align the model with human preferences and safety requirements.
The LLaMA tokenizer uses byte pair encoding with a vocabulary of 32000 tokens balancing coverage of diverse languages and computational efficiency.
LLaMA models outperform GPT-3 on most benchmarks despite being significantly smaller demonstrating the importance of training data quality over model size.
Diffusion models learn to generate data by gradually denoising a sample from a Gaussian noise distribution through a learned reverse diffusion process.
The forward diffusion process adds Gaussian noise to data over many timesteps until the data becomes indistinguishable from pure random noise.
Stable diffusion uses a latent diffusion model that operates in a compressed latent space reducing computational cost while maintaining generation quality.
Classifier-free guidance improves sample quality in diffusion models by training with and without class conditioning and interpolating at inference time.
DALL-E 2 uses a diffusion model conditioned on CLIP image embeddings to generate high quality images from natural language text descriptions.
Reinforcement learning from human feedback trains a reward model on human preference data then uses PPO to optimize the language model against this reward.
InstructGPT applies RLHF to align GPT-3 with human intent producing outputs that are more helpful honest and harmless than the base model.
The reward model is trained as a binary classifier on pairs of model outputs labeled by human annotators as preferred or not preferred.
Constitutional AI uses a set of principles to guide model behavior during RLHF training replacing some human feedback with AI-generated feedback signals.
Direct preference optimization simplifies RLHF by directly optimizing the language model on preference data without training a separate reward model.
LoRA adapts large language models by injecting trainable low-rank matrices into transformer layers keeping original weights frozen to reduce memory cost.
The rank decomposition in LoRA represents weight updates as a product of two small matrices greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters.
QLoRA combines LoRA with 4-bit quantization of the base model enabling fine-tuning of 65B parameter models on a single GPU with minimal quality loss.
LoRA adapters can be merged back into the base model weights at inference time resulting in zero additional latency compared to the original model.
Adapter methods like LoRA achieve comparable performance to full fine-tuning on most benchmarks while training less than one percent of total parameters.
Vision transformer applies the transformer architecture directly to image patches by splitting images into fixed-size patches and treating them as tokens.
ViT requires large scale pre-training data to outperform convolutional networks but achieves state of the art results when trained on datasets like JFT-300M.
Patch embeddings in ViT are created by linearly projecting flattened image patches into the transformer input dimension similar to word embeddings in NLP.
DeiT improves ViT data efficiency through knowledge distillation from a CNN teacher allowing transformers to be trained on ImageNet without extra data.
Swin transformer introduces hierarchical feature maps and shifted window attention enabling efficient processing of high resolution images for dense tasks.
GraphRAG builds a knowledge graph from documents to enable graph-guided retrieval that captures relationships between entities across multiple source documents.
Community detection in GraphRAG identifies clusters of related entities allowing the system to summarize entire topics rather than individual document chunks.
Graph traversal in GraphRAG uses Cypher queries on Neo4j to find relevant subgraphs that connect query entities through multi-hop relationship paths.
GraphRAG outperforms naive RAG on questions requiring synthesis of information from multiple documents by leveraging entity relationships in the knowledge graph.
The hybrid retrieval in GraphRAG blends vector similarity search with graph-guided expansion to improve both recall and answer grounding with citations.
